title: Is enterprise open-source LLM spend really falling?
marker: Claim: open source is eating enterprise / 11% vs 19%
note: Validated the article claim against Menlo, Hugging Face, OpenRouter/a16z, Linux Foundation, TechCrunch, and current market reporting.
synthesis: The paradox is that the story people tell says open source is winning, but one important enterprise metric says open source share is falling. That does not necessarily mean open source is losing everywhere. Menlo's 11% from 19% claim is directionally supported for U.S. enterprise foundation-model API market share, but it is not a clean measure of all enterprise AI spend. The reconciliation is scope: closed models dominate enterprise API dollars and regulated procurement, while open models are growing in developer ecosystems, startups, cost-sensitive workloads, and self-hosted/specialized production.

interaction.content: Go and spend a good deal of time trying to validate or invalidate the claim: “The prevailing story right now is that open source is eating the enterprise... open source models just fell to 11% of enterprise LLM spend, down from 19% a year ago.” Deep background websearch.

Researched Menlo Ventures 2024, mid-year 2025, and December 2025 enterprise reports; Hugging Face Spring 2026 open-source ecosystem data; OpenRouter/a16z 100T-token usage study; Linux Foundation open-source AI adoption research; TechCrunch coverage of Menlo; and current market reporting on AI cost pressure. Working conclusion: Menlo supports the directional enterprise API-share claim, with important methodology caveats; broader ecosystem data supports the counter-narrative that open-weight models are growing quickly outside large-enterprise API spend.
What exactly is the paradox? Is it that the narrative says open source is winning, but numbers say open source is losing?

Yes, that's the basic paradox, with one precision: the numbers do not prove open source is losing overall. They show open source losing share in a specific enterprise LLM API/spend metric, while the broader narrative says open source is gaining fast in capability, developer adoption, startup usage, and some production workloads. The puzzle is why both can be true at once. The answer is scope and maturity: new enterprise use cases spend on frontier closed models, while mature or cost-sensitive workloads are where open models tend to grow. Check-back: if open models are used heavily for cheap high-volume tasks, but closed models are used for expensive frontier tasks, which side might win on usage volume and which side might win on dollar spend?

title: How should an audio tutor walk through this article?
note: Prompt for section-by-section audio tutoring with near-verbatim reading.
synthesis: Use the tutor prompt to read each logical section of Jesse Zhang's article almost verbatim, then add brief plain-English context and ask whether the user is ready to continue.

interaction.content: Create a prompt that breaks this article into logical sections, adds more context/colour, and has another agent talk me through each section in plain terms over audio. Ensure every section is read almost verbatim.

Created a resource-level tutor prompt that preserves the article section by section, instructs the next agent to read each section almost verbatim before explaining it, and requires an explicit ready-to-move-on check after every section.
